questions.question: Explain the concept of Analysis Modeling in requirements engineering. List and briefly describe the five primary components of an analysis model.
keyPoints: Analysis modeling creates abstract system descriptions from user perspective
Five components: Scenario-based (use cases), Class-based (object analysis), Behavioral (state/sequence), Flow-oriented (DFDs), Data modeling (ERD)
Each component provides different视角 of the system
Components work together to form complete requirements specification

questions.question: What is requirements traceability? Differentiate between forward traceability and backward traceability. Why is traceability important in large software projects?
keyPoints: Forward traceability: requirements → design → code → test
Backward traceability: design/code → requirements
Importance: impact analysis, change management, verification coverage, regulatory compliance
Helps identify missing or unverifiable requirements

questions.question: Describe the differences between Aggregation, Composition, and Generalization relationships in class-based modeling. Provide a suitable example for each.
keyPoints: Aggregation: weak 'has-a', parts can exist independently (e.g., Department has Teachers)
Composition: strong 'has-a', parts cannot exist without whole (e.g., Book has Pages)
Generalization: 'is-a' inheritance relationship (e.g., SavingsAccount is a BankAccount)
Multiplicity and lifecycle dependencies distinguish these

questions.question: Explain the purpose of CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaboration) cards in requirements analysis. How do they help in identifying classes and their interactions?
keyPoints: CRC cards are index cards for object-oriented analysis
Three sections: Class name, Responsibilities, Collaborators
Facilitate team brainstorming and discussion
Help identify classes, their roles, and relationships early in analysis

questions.question: What is the difference between Functional and Non-Functional requirements? Provide two examples of each in the context of a Hospital Management System.
keyPoints: Functional: specific behaviors system must perform
Non-functional: quality attributes (performance, security, etc.)
Functional example: 'System shall allow doctors to view patient records'
Non-functional example: 'System shall respond to queries within 2 seconds'

questions.question: Explain the elements of a Use Case diagram. How do you differentiate between Basic Flow and Alternative Flows in use case descriptions?
keyPoints: Elements: Actors, Use Cases, System boundary, Relationships
Basic Flow: primary successful path (happy path)
Alternative Flows: alternate paths for exceptions/errors
Alternative flows include: error conditions, alternate inputs, system failures

questions.question: What are Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)? Explain the four fundamental components of DFD with appropriate symbols.
keyPoints: DFD represents data movement through system
Four components: Process (circle/rounded), Data Store (open rectangle), External Entity (square), Data Flow (arrow)
Shows transformation of input to output
Supports top-down decomposition (Context → Level 1 → Level 2)

questions.question: You are assigned to develop a Requirements Model for an Online Library Management System. Using scenario-based, class-based, and behavioral modeling approaches, demonstrate how you would analyze and document the 'Book Borrowing' use case. Include relevant class relationships, state transitions, and DFD elements.
keyPoints: Use Case: Borrow Book - Actor (Member), Preconditions (valid membership), Basic Flow (search → select → reserve → collect)
Class Diagram: Member, Book, Librarian, LoanRecord with appropriate multiplicities
State Diagram: Book states (Available → Reserved → Borrowed → Returned)
DFD: Context diagram showing system boundary, major processes for borrowing workflow
CRC Cards for key classes
Relationships: Member borrows Book (1..* to 1..*), Librarian manages Loan

questions.question: A project has 60 requirements. After analysis, 12 are marked as 'Verified' and linked to test cases, 18 are linked to design documents but not test cases, 8 have no traceability. Calculate the traceability matrix coverage. What actions should be taken to improve traceability? Justify your answer with calculations.
keyPoints: Total requirements: 60
Requirements with test case linkage: 12
Requirements with design linkage only: 18
Requirements with no linkage: 8
Test coverage: 12/60 × 100 = 20%
Design coverage: (12+18)/60 × 100 = 50%
Gap analysis: 8 requirements completely untraced

Actions: Create traceability links for all 8 untraced, generate test cases for 18 design-linked requirements, establish traceability matrix template, enforce traceability discipline in project processes

questions.question: Compare and contrast Data Flow Diagrams (DFD) and Object-Oriented (OO) modeling approaches. Under what circumstances would you recommend using each approach for requirements modeling?
keyPoints: DFD: process-oriented, shows data flow, good for transformation-heavy systems
OO: object-oriented, shows structure and behavior, good for complex domain models
DFD advantages: intuitive for functional decomposition, clear data flow
OO advantages: maps to code, supports encapsulation, better for complex behaviors
DFD suitable for: transaction processing systems, data transformation workflows
OO suitable for: complex business domains, interactive systems, long-lived systems
Hybrid approach often used in practice
